content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Favorite comedy movie?

**Score:** 32
**Body:** Looking for a good comedy movie where I’m laughing the WHOLE time!!! I really like silly, funny movies like Zoolander, Step-Brothers, Hot Rod or early Adam Sandler movies. Just want a good laugh tonight so I’m open to any suggestions!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Airplane 
2. Office Space
3. Tropic Thunder, the dialogue is too funny
4. Mean girls
5. Grandmas Boy
6. Wet Hot American Summer. 

They came together. 

Black dynamite. 
7. Spinal Tap 
8. Better Off Dead. One of the greatest 80s comedies there is.
9. Top Secret!

So many jokes crammed in there you have to watch it several times to get them all
10. The Big Lebowski 

## 2. Looking for more movies like 'About Time'

**Score:** 29
**Body:** About Time is my favorite movie and I'm looking for films with a similar warm, light-hearted feel where there aren't really any major conflicts but is instead about people just learning to enjoy and appreciate their lives? Doesn't necessarily have to be a rom-com  

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Chef
2. Enchanted April. It's quiet and lovely and about the very things you mentioned. 
3. The Map of Tiny Perfect Things is really lovely 
4. Amelie!
5. Click is the closest film I have seen to About Time (but it’s not as good).

Other films that appreciate the joy of life and have a similar feel and production quality:
- The Intouchables (French, not untouchables)
- The World’s Fastest Indian (New Zealand)
- 18 Years Later (Italy)

Also these famous films…
- Love Actually
- Along Came Polly
- The Secret Life of Walter Mitty
6. Before sunrise
7. It's a dark comedy, but Harold and Maude might be something along those lines.  
8. Safety not guaranteed is a good one!
9. Mr Holland’s Opus 
10. Warm, light-hearted - I love *Under the Tuscan Sun*. I love all the side characters and the growth of the relationships between them and the main character, played by Diane Lane.  

I also like *Shall We Dance* with Richard Gere and Jennifer Lopez. Not really a fan of hers but I am willing to tolerate her in this because again the side characters are all wonderful and I love seeing the friendships develop between them and Richard Gere's character.  

Both of these films share similar themes around people learning to enjoy and appreciate their lives.

## 3. Hated romance movies, Changed my mind after watching before sunrise.

**Score:** 28
**Body:** So as a dude I was always super turned off by romances. My ex’s favorite movie is 500 days of summer so unfortunately this genre has kind of caught lot of undeserving flack from me. I recently watched before sunrise and it quite literally floored me it was unbelievable. Any romance recommendations? 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The sequels are good.  Before Sunset (2004) and Before Midnight (2013).  
  - OP please trust this comment, they are that good
2. Atonement is pretty epic. And I'm not a fan of period stuff really. 

All of us strangers is basically perfect. 
  - Atonement is a great rec. 

Similar in that they’re not a straightforward romance - Eternal Sunshine and Lost in Translation. 

    - Eternal Sunshine is excellent.
3. About Time. Very layered, romantic, but also touches on many themes. One of the best films  ever made in my opinion!
4. If you want to try a cute fun one that's a story inside a movie, The Princess Bride is a classic and is comfortable making fun of itself in good taste. 
5. True Romance  (1993)
6. When Harry Met Sally 

7. While You Were Sleeping 

Shakespeare in Love

The Green Card 


## 4. Best Documentaries?

**Score:** 19
**Body:** I'm looking to get more into documentaries. I'm a huge fan of true stories and I don't want documentaries that are super scary. Give me a long list of documentaries you truly enjoyed!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 20 Feet From Stardom:  Winner of the 2014 Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature, this film looks at the world of backup vocalists and the legends they support.
2. 13th (2016)

Dear Zachary (2008)

Searching for Sugarman (2012)

The Imposter (2012)

Three Identical Strangers (2018)

Blackfish (2013)

The Central Park Five (2012)

Man on a Wire (2008)

20 Feet From Stardom (2013)

3. Paris Is Burning

Hoop Dreams

The Last Waltz

20 Feet From Stardom

Searching for Sugar Man

Exit Through the Gift Shop

Blackfish

Citizenfour

13th

Wild Wild Country

Three Identical Strangers

Long Strange Trip

Don’t Fuck with Cats

Jesus Camp

This is a variety of documentary genres and should be easy to find streaming or on YouTube.
4. The Alpinist and The Dark Wizard 
5. Icarus
6. Grizzly man, and anything by the director, Herzog. 

The thin blue line. 

If you’re in the sports, watch 30 for 30. 
7. Meru

Free Solo 

Valley Uprising 

Dirtbag 
8. The Times of Harvey Milk (1984)

Won the Oscar for Best Documentary. I prefer it greatly over Milk.
9. 2011 PBS Radioactive Wolves: Wolves and other wildlife thrive in the "dead zone" surrounding the remains of the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant.
10. I am watching Class Action Park right now, its good.

## 5. Movies to watch just before sleeping

**Score:** 19
**Body:** As the title says, I want to have the best sleep after watching the movie.Just recommend everything u got. I will watch the catchy ones and add the other films to my watchlist. Don't rec serious stuffs excluding romance. 

Rom com,dark com, comedy ✅

Hoping to see your recs 

Thanks in advance :D

Edit: I have seen pretty much basic movies 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Red Turtle is a dialogue-free vibe of a movie and just a nice one to drift away with.
2. Lord of the Rings trilogy 
  - Im on rewatch:)
3. NGL Twilight is a fantastic series to fall asleep to. Its the blue hues and moody music. Same with Donnie Darko
  - Not watched twilight, thanks
4. While You Were Sleeping
5. Jumpstreet franchise.
6. P2 , 2007
7. I used to fall asleep to Pirates of the Caribbean 
8. The Fountain 

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Tim Roth turns 65 today. One of the most talented British actors, indeed. Happy birthday to him!

**Score:** 2245

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Rosencrantz and Guildenstern by Tom Stoppard with him and Gary Oldman was a true masterpiece. He nailed it in Reservoir Dogs, Pulp Fiction, Four Rooms and a lot of another good movies
  - Also Rob Roy! 
  - Tom Stoppard is sorely missed. Such a hilarious writer. 

"There are no more than two or three poets of the first rank now living, and I will not shoot one of them dead over a perpendicular poke in a gazebo with a woman whose reputation could not be adequately defended with a platoon of musketry deployed by rota."
2. Lie to me!
  - Loved that show
    - They could've easily got 2 or 3 more seasons out of it.
3. Tim Roth Happy Birthday 🎂

![gif](giphy|gnFXgKBmLJXxe)
4. https://preview.redd.it/bw9nyhjvu31h1.jpeg?width=1101&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=5cd5b12d5ed7edfc5254c9560f9eefabde7024ea


5. Met him once at an art show he curated in Los Angeles. The wine was good.
6. Happy birthday Honey Bunny!

## 2. Nolan making the A-list cast slum it in Sicily is the ultimate power move

**Score:** 961
**Body:** Let's be real, Nolan could get away with filming this thing on the moon if he wanted to. But the fact that he's making all these huge stars stay in 'budget accommodation' just proves how focused he is. He's not about the Hollywood fluff; it's all about the film. Hathaway says it keeps egos in check and the money on the screen, and I'm here for it. It's a reminder that the work matters more than the trailers, the premieres, or the fancy hotels. Sets the tone for the entire production, I reckon.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. “My trailer air conditioner only had knobs with four settings.”
  - They only change the towels at the hotel every *other* day! It's basically a shelter 
  - Meanwhile Nolan's goes to 11.
2. I can’t tell if this is a parody of a crazy Nolan fan praising him
3. I like Anne Hathaway but she is paid far more money per movie than I will earn over the course of my entire lifetime. A couple of months on a budget working holiday in Italy does not make us similar. 
  - I stay 10+ nights a month for work in truly budget accommodations for at least a factor less than they make. I get you.
4. ![gif](giphy|lRZjlasctAcvu)
5. “Slumming it in Sicily”….. let us know when they are slumming it in the backwoods of Mississippi or West Virginia.
6. this was nowhere near a white lotus experience basta !
7. For the record, my wife and I slummed it in Sicily for our honeymoon and we never wanted to leave. 

## 3. Interstellar Or Project Hail Mary ?

**Score:** 552

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Do you want to cry because you're sad or because you're happy?
  - Happy
    - Hail Mary.
2. Better question would be Martian v Project Hail Mary 
  - Same book author, yes.  But the "one way trip to deep space to save the Earth" plot of Project Hail Mary and Interstellar are more similar.
  - Better question would be My Favorite Martian vs Predator 
    - ![gif](giphy|3o6ZsXxK0TerLizZpm)
3. They’re very different
4. Both very good. I go with Interstellar for the more serious tone.
5. Interstellar feels more...authentic? Maybe that's a tremendously pretentious thing to say, but so be it.

PHM technically might have fewer 'holes', but I feel Interstellar just hits its emotional points more effectively.

## 4. What is the best bad movie every made?

**Score:** 515
**Body:** Road House (1989) has to take the cake as the best "bad" movie ever made.

It takes itself so seriously while serving up a smorgasbord of cheese, cringe, and laughably hilarious action scenes that are so outrageous, they're actually good.

Every cringe line of dialogue is a classic, quotable work of low art. Every fight is the time of your life in entertainment. The mythology of a "famous bouncer" who not only is so good that bar owners scout him out a thousand miles away, but has a mentor who is also a legend, is brilliant.

The villains are so humanly corrupt and evil that you believe they could be at the next farm town over with connections to JC Penny.

Swayze delivers the best performance of his career, and Bruce Lee would've loved it. Add in the "beautiful doctor" he seduces, and it's the ultimate machismo fantasy that will remain a classic forever.

You can watch this movie on repeat for hours and never get bored (I say that because I've seen some channels literally run it back to back in recent years).

Wish they still made them like this.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There is an “over the top” quality that was pure 80’s gold. Commando I think is on the list. Roadhouse is too. I don’t think they’re bad. Just…ya know… a lot. Lol
  - Now that you mention it… Over the Top was a great bad movie. A guy drank gasoline to get amped up, and a magic hat turn that powers Sly up. 
2. I always thought the gold went to Con Air.
  - That's definitely top 5, maybe 2nd imo.
3. ![gif](giphy|y1eljNRzOtOvYEpy6w)
4. Gone in 60 seconds

![gif](giphy|Qw4X3FnmFFCPANtlhtK)
  - Nicolas Cage is low-hanging fruit for this question.
5. Bloodsport should be up there 
6. I unabashedly adore this movie. 12/10
7. Hard Ticket to Hawaii for me. But it's genuinely one of my favorite movies to watch. 

But original Roadhouse is also great. Love that movie. 

## 5. What is a movie in which the actor's performance was better than the movie itself?

**Score:** 304
**Body:** Val Kilmer in The Doors. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. James McAvoy Split
  - So much potential 
2. Raul Julia streetfighter 
3. John Goodman in 10 Cloverfield lane. 

Not a bad movie at all but it absolutely was elevated by his performance. 
4. Val Kilmer completely owns The Doors his Jim Morrison is so electric it makes the movie way better than it has any right to be.


5. Lincoln with Daniel Day Lewis
6. Joaquin Phoenix in Joker.
7. Most biopics.
8. Jim Carey Man on the Moon
9. Christian Bale - Thor:  Love and Thunder 

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. The Handmaiden….wow.

**Score:** 154
**Body:** I recently went on a Park Chan-wook binge which culminated with The Handmaiden……surely this is considered his masterpiece? 

Structurally, the film is incredible effective in the way it employs it’s twists. Once the first twist arrives, it’s impossible to not be invested. 

Obviously it’s abrasive approach to sex is confronting, but it didn’t seem exploitative. It seemed to be essential for exploring the sexualisation of women under the male gaze in a patriarchal context, but also to highlight the fundamental difference between infatuation and genuine affection. 

And thematically, it seemed a beautiful historical recreation of a period rife with power dynamics that two women attempt to transcend. And for all his disturbing creations and his effortless ability at creating fundamentally icky characters, the Uncle has to be his most sinister creation. 

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I go back and forth between The Handmaiden and Decision to Leave as his masterpiece. Give DtL a shot. I thought No Other Choice was good but ranked lower than either of these for sure.
  - decision to leave is so stunning. a meditative fever dream 
2. I think Oldboy will always be considered his masterpiece but the handmaiden is definitely in the top tier of his work. There was controversy regarding the explicit scenes in the movie but i found all that nonsensical. The acting, production design, music, the story itself all was great in the movie.
  - Regarding the explicit scenes, this was one of the more memorable reviews from a Christian movie site:


>The Handmaiden is a godless, pornographic, perverted, radical feminist, lesbian story.


🤌
    - > The Handmaiden is a godless, pornographic, perverted, radical feminist, lesbian story.

But it also has flaws.
    - What an endorsement! 
  - People go on about the notion of "unnecessary sex scenes" in movies, which makes no more sense than unnecessary action scenes or unnecessary dancing scenes or literally anything else whose main purpose is to have aesthetic value in itself, but also... this is one of the movies in which I most felt the absolute, life-burning, will-kill-or-die-for-you passion between the two leads, *because* of those scenes. They made it really viscerally clear how strong their love was (by making us feel all hot and bothered with them in a way that was hard to forget).

3. I just finished Lady Vengeance and I might’ve been premature in saying that the ‘uncle’ was wook’s most ickiest character. The last 30 minutes was some of the most brutal cinema that I’ve ever watched. 
4. Very succinct and incisive analysis OP! Your points are exactly why I consider The Handmaiden Park Chan-wook's most accomplished film as well. Both his writer and production designer for the film are women which I think adds to the film's tackling of female sexuality and the exploitative nature of the patriarchy. His co-writer Jeong Seo-kyeong also co-wrote Thirst, I'm a Cyborg, Lady Vengeance and Decision to Leave with him and edited Stoker while his production designer Ryu Seong-Hie has worked with him since Oldboy and are two of his most long time collaborators and therefore very influential on him as a director (as he is very collaborative filmmaker. 
  - It’s also based on a novel written by a gay woman 

## 2. I miss the sound quality of dialogue in older films

**Score:** 127
**Body:** I'm doing a full run of Cassavetes filmography and it got me thinking about how dialogue recording in particular has evolved over the years. 

There's something nostalgic about those peaks in the audio when an actor raises there voice. And the differences in Dbs when dialogue is captured from a character off screen, a bit distant from the boom mic. 

Clearly current microphones/editing technology have weeded out that method of recording coupled with the fact that a lot of films today utilize ADR in post. I feel we've lost a human element that's when you level the playing field sonically. Anyways go watch A Killing of a Chinese Bookie

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You are incorrect about current films using a lot of adr in. Post.  Current Films use less than ever and often just a few lines.  Audio software has gotten much better at removing noise and reverb from recordings, and lav mics  have also gotten better at sounding closer to boom mics.  Also software is used to combine lav and boom mics without phasing.  
  - Also the main complaint that people have about audio in modern movies is the high dynamic range- what OP is saying he loves about old movies. Back then there was a much smaller range, which meant dialogue was clearer on most speakers systems
    - Open question, is the old timey low dynamic range a consequence of style or technical limitations?

I'm speculating here...

What seems to be the change is the theater's trend to crazy loud action stuff.  If old timey theaters couldn't push that kind of volume, any mix with explosion @ 0db and dialog @ -60db would have really unbalanced mixes.

(I don't know the lingo for mix in film, I'm using audio mix lingo which will be intelligible to film mix nerds but not necessarily this sub)
      - Imo 80% style, 20% technology.

So I mix audio for my job but I'm absolutely not an expert, that rabbit hole runs deep. But I cut my teeth on documentaries and TV shows and my target dynamic range is 6db between the highs and lows of dialogue, and compress the dialogue as such.

But when I drop a 5.1 surround movie from last year on to my editing timeline, the dialogue can be 15-20db range. Super loud highs, super quiet lows.

Now this next part is a bit theory,  but follow me: back in the 1970s, mixing was done analogue and for home TVs that were basic af. So mixing your movie was more of a simple operation of "make sure they can hear it." 

Now? We have home theaters,  but also we have another element- studios trying to make it worth going to the theater. So they create audio mixes that damn near *must* be played on a good system, along with color grading that damn near *must* be watched on a shiny near 75" QLED.
        - You're saying some stuff that's over my head, I'm an amateur musuc producer who's recently dived into tryhard buffing my mixes.

Like, soon as you mention 5:1, I know what that means superficially,  but I don't have an intimate understanding of what that means in practice, I'm pretty shit at stereo design, I know a few tricks but when swimming in those waters I stay in the shallow end, I'm not Michael Phelps.

The 1970s(etc) time reference is interesting.   Seeing how mixes were done back in the day with those huge analog banks, 64, 128, 256 channels, but only a level, hi shelf, low shelf, band pass, and an analog meter and a really good set of ears...

Such a different process!  And the tension between old timey mix process and digital mix process with precise metering and eq and an armory of fx ready at the hip....

I nod vigorously at the "loudness wars" element/parallel.
      - The biggest limitation was the optical soundtrack that was used on release prints. It had a very small range of loudness. Read Walter Murch's latest book for a detailed description.
  - Additionally, actors in older films had a different style of speaking to accommodate for the technology of the time. Today actors can get away with mumbling and speaking so so softly, but back then it was important for an actor to speak a little louder and with very clear annunciation so the tape could capture the dialogue in an understandable way. I think that’s also a big complaint folks have about modern films’ dialogue and sound mixing. 
  - I was under the impression that ADR is still used rather heavily, especially when it comes to big budget blockbusters and anything with loud setpieces, but not only.

Has there actually been a noticeable decline in its use?
2. what you're hearing with those peaks is that the tape machines and field mixers of that era had a limited amount of headroom, and could distort if the levels were set too high when the actor was giving a dynamic performance. 

I agree there's something lost there sonically, because hearing the sound blow out can give more impact to someone yelling, particularly in horror movies where screams had this kind of "ripping a hole in the fabric of the film" quality.

there are a number of quite good tape emulation plugins that are commonly used in music production, but Mank is the only film I'm aware of that's used it to a similar effect. it's always a little odd to my ears when something is shot on 16mm but then the sound is crisp and bright, and I really wish more films would use tape emulation to soften the edges of the sound.
3. Most modern sound design is bland and uninteresting, just over-produced with a focus on flawless perfection. It lacks the natural feeling that was inherent with analogue recording.

I'm not saying the world should go back to strictly analogue, not at all, but the human voice needs more care. As it is, the vocal tracks take second stage to over-produced ambience and music. Again, this is just my opinion based on having watched films since I was in short pants forty years ago.

I find it so boring how much narrative is shot medium and medium-wide and the sound design emphasizes pointless ambience in scenes, as if the room tone is cranked. Also, the SFX use such a heavy hand, as if the sound of footsteps or ice in a glass or thumping music in a nightclub is the most important element in a scene.

I think it's probably due to the TV-ification of dramatic content, giving everything the visual depth of a soap opera, but crammed with as much ambience and SFX possible, and make sure every actor has perfectly-coiffed and sprayed hair, as if it's a Hallmark christmas movie. 

Canadian Television has always been like this, just mind-numbingly bland. It's even worse now. If you really want to see what I mean, take a look at a show called Sullivan's Crossing. It's shockingly awful in every way.

Please give me back Cinema with a feeling of expansiveness and sound with depth and subtlety, movies that invite you into a world and a mood.

Sorry for the rant. Obviously, this doesn't apply to everything, there are some incredible things being made, but they are getting lost in the sea of bland.

## 3. am i the only one who thinks the ending of Poor Things is actually super cynical? (not a feminist masterpiece)

**Score:** 78
**Body:** everyone keeps hyping up this movie as the ultimate story about freedom and female emancipation. but honestly? i feel like people completely missed the point of the ending.
Bella didn't find "freedom". she just climbed the class ladder. like, think about it... she starts off as literal property in that mansion. then she goes to paris and basically joins the working class as a sex worker. then on the cruise ship she has that whole emotional breakdown about poverty and inequality.
but how does it actually end? she just goes right back, inherits godwin's massive estate, and becomes the exact same wealthy elite that just sits around drinking tea in a garden. she didn't break any system. she just used old money to buy her way into the 1%. kinda weird that everyone is praising this as some huge liberation tbh. she literally just became a rich snob.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think the problem is trying to label it as the 'ultimate' version of anything. If you view the ending as a pure black-and-white moral victory or a total sell-out, you’re kind of missing the point. The ending is intentionally ambiguous. Bella doesn’t dismantle capitalism, but she does claim autonomy over her own life, which is a massive shift from where she started. Whether that makes her 'liberated' or just a 'benevolent elite' is exactly the conversation the movie wants us to have. Any story that leaves you debating the ethics of its hero hours later is doing its job, it’s not supposed to be a clean, 'perfect' manifesto
2. Everything is pretty cynical if you hold it to a revolutionary standard. Most people are working towards self-respect and respect from peers, which is by itself a pretty difficult goal to achieve and one that many never do in their lifetime. To move from being property to holding a certain degree of self-actualization is definitely a victory for the individual character. Unfortunately it's not a victory for the proletariat but not many stories are.
  - For real. “They didn’t vanquish post-industrial capitalism in this movie, so that means it’s jaded.” Like, come on now. 
3. My problem with the movie is that it's about a woman who develops rapidly but it gets all hung up on her sexual liberation. It only pays lip services to her political life beyond that and I really wished it had continued on until she was older to see how much more she developed intellectually. It rare that I want a movie to have less sex, but Poor Things really could have used less sex. 
  - *Furious Jumping*
4. Why is your argument framed as a rebuttal of a vague nonspecific "everyone" who is saying something? Can you be more specific about what exactly is the position you are arguing against?
5. My biggest issue with the ending is that Bella’s final act in a movie all about discovering her own agency is to violently strip that agency away from someone else and the film does not even try to interrogate the ramifications of that.  It treats it like a fairy tail happy ending despite it undermining the whole point of Bella’s journey but tries ting it as fine because the guy she did it to “deserved it.”


I largely like this film but wow does the ending leave a bad taste in my mouth in a way that I don’t remotely believe it meant to.
  - I didn't read the film as having a  straightforward happy ending. It does imitate the form of a fairy tale happy ending, but it feels pretty ambiguous to me (as another commenter said). 

It feels actually more like the ending of a like actual fairy tale as opposed to a sanitized modern fairu tale - like yes a bad guy is defeated and the heroine ends up in control of her life in a mansion with a loving husband, but there's a clear element of the grotesque and absurd. Also the music to me just does not come across as simply straightforwardly happy, it sets a tone containing triumph and success but not ONLY those things.
6. Agree. They feel cynical because once stripped to their core there is not much being said. I think Lanthimos is clearly a capable director, but to me he combines the worst qualities of Lars von Trier and Wes Anderson, while lacking the genuine inventiveness of either. It is also clear he wants his films to be perceived as strange and controversial, yet they always feel “safe weird”, as if as if he was simultaneously trying not to alienate anyone. There is nothing surreal or thought-provoking in them beyond surface-level zaniness and violence.

Compare that, for instance, to David Lynch, who worked within the Hollywood system and always faced a ton of studio backlash, but still managed to produce highly idiosyncratic, baffling films that generated extensive critical interpretation, essays, books and so on. Lanthimos is just empty provocation that barely even provokes.
7. I haven't seen Bugonia yet, but I feel like Yorgos Lanthimos' best era was when he was working with a bit more external restraint, specificaly when he just started making slightly more "hollywood" films -  the Lobster, the Favourite, Killing of a Sacred Deer. Imo he has a tendency to get way too self-indulgent when left to his own devices and there's no-one to reign back his more egregious quirks so his more "auteur" work are mostly my least favourite - Dogtooth from earlier in his career and his recent batch of films starting with Poor Things where he is succesfull enough that he gets more of a carte blanche and a very notable budget. (but this is probably an unpopular opinion).

## 4. The Blair Witch Project is pretty good

**Score:** 27
**Body:** I post this because everytime i see someone talking about this movie they talk about how influential it is or how boring it is. But genuineli i really enjoy it. Maybe it is because thats the movie i tend to like but i love the dialogue centric it is. Like to me seeing the character slowly getting tired and disucussing all the time for random shit made me feel a Lot more inmersed in the story. In fact I would Say that the most terrifying thing of the movie is not Witch but the feeling of being in a forest all alone and not knowing where to go. Idk it felt very real and very well maded. Not to Say the Witch itself was not scary. Not showing her was a very good decision and since until now the horror was mostly realistic seeing how much it fuck with the characters mind was very cool. Of course most of this is achieve with the "acting" which in a sense it's no acting at all which is again a very clever and unique decision. And the ending was very cool too. It feels very surreal in a cool way.

Idk i really enjoy it and i don't get why i don't see more praise for the movie. The copycats were and are anoying but the original still stands as a very cool and unique movie.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I only watched it for the first time a couple of weeks ago. Thought it was great, even better after watching the documentary about how it was made. A shame how they did the actors like that but still a great watch. There's a 3 hour cut out there that adds all the additional cut footage so I'm looking forward to that! 
  - A documentary? 
    - The documentary is called The Blair Witch Project. It's three college film students filming a documentary about the local legend of the blair witch.
2. There are some naysayers but it’s broadly a very well received movie. Absolutely smashed both commercially and critically on release. Maybe you’re seeing some pushback against the hype in a vacuum, because parts of it haven’t aged well for modern audiences (though I agree on the whole that it’s still great). 

It’s sort of a victim of its own popularity. A lot of what made it really good and unique became immediately mainstream as a result of its success, so going into it already used to modern norms can make it hit less hard. 
  - I was kinda making an hyperbole. But for example on letterboxd it has a 3.3 and i feel like it is at least a 3.5. of course 3.3 is not bad but idk i dont see a lot of praise for the movie. I see mostly people talking about things outside the movie 
3. Watched in theater here in Italy and scared the shit

We where actually much impressed about the found footage technique and mostly believed the hype

I clearly remember there was a website you could visit and gave you the impression the story war real 
4. Yes, it bothers me too how much of the discourse surrounding the movie is about the marketing at the time of its release, how some people actually thought it was real, etc. Set all that aside and it’s still just a really good movie.
  - Agreed, but I cannot express enough how ubiquitous the lead in to the movie was. It was everywhere. There were people advocacy groups boycotting the movie, saying it was a snuff film, parents were banning their children from seeing it, it felt like everyone was debating whether it was real or not. It was pretty revolutionary, and for something like that you can't really replicate it and it's never been done to the same scale since. 

The movie scared the hell out of people before they even saw it, and then the movie actually delivered. 
5. I feel confident saying that I don’t think we’ll ever see a more hysterically hyped up movie like this (non- capes/franchise) ever again. 

I was trying to describe the literal theatrics and pre-release hype this movie caused to some buddies who are a few years younger and it’s hard to capture. What a time. 
6. For the most part, any negativity, or denying the film of its due, usually seems to be a reaction to the overly fawning praise, and the ubiquity in conversation it held for a while. 

Basically, whenever something’s awesome, some people are going to love to demonstrate how unique they are by declaring how much it actually sucks 😄

And then of course there are people who just don’t like that sort of movie, but that opinion is so..controversial? (not really *controversial*, it’s just not the general consensus), that it gets a lot of attention, 

and we know that attention leads to views and engagement and so over time there’s been a false impression that many or *most* people feel negatively about the film,

when really as someone who was there since the unique marketing campaign that preceded the film and also heard what the people were saying after it came out,

I do think it’s generally accepted to be a hell of a good film. 

It also by the way suffers a little bit these days bc all of its tricks have been aped to death; it will never be fresh to new audiences the way it was to us. 

Nonetheless, usually people watching it for the first time really seem to get why it’s one of the greats!

## 5. Was there any update on The Magnificent Ambersons search?

**Score:** 14
**Body:** Question, Was there any update on The Magnificent Ambersons search?

Back in 2021, Joshua Grossberg embarked on a search to find the original cut of The Magnificent Ambersons because Allegedly when Orson Welles was in Brazil shooting It's All True, he received a full print of The Magnificent Ambersons for him to edit. So far, the print hasn't surface and many feel it was destroyed, but many think it has survived and was potentially sold to a private collector.

When I heard Grossberg was leading a search for the footage after 2021, my reaction was, "Good Luck". I think Magnificent Amberson is an interesting film by Orson Welles and even in its compromised state, I still think its a masterpiece. Anyway, on Grossberg's search, I think the last time he did an update was in 2024, where he was still chasing leads and possibly found a person who claim to have saw the original cut in the 60s. But that's it, so I was wondering if there were any updates since then.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I seriously hope so. It's only my own belief, but it's very likely that, if we had the intended cut, nowadays we would be talking more about The Magnificent Ambersons than of Citizen Kanze. With half the intended material, the film is still an absolute masterpiece, with some clear omissions that deter it from being superior to CK.
2. Most of the missing footage from The Magnificent Ambersons is almost certainly gone for good, and it’s one of the great tragedies in movie history.

Orson Welles originally made a much longer, darker version of the film. Depending on the cut, it was somewhere around two hours or more. But while Welles was away in Brazil working on another project, the studio -  RKO Pictures - took the movie away from him after some test audiences reacted badly to it.

The studio panicked. They thought the film was too bleak and slow, especially for wartime audiences, so they hacked out something like 40 to 50 minutes, reshaped the story, and even shot a new ending that was much more upbeat than what Welles had intended. The version that got released was only about 88 minutes long.

As for the missing footage itself, it was probably just destroyed. Back then, studios didn’t really think about film preservation the way people do now. Old nitrate film was bulky, flammable, and expensive to store, so deleted scenes were often thrown out once the final version of a movie was locked in. The removed Ambersons footage seems to have met that fate sometime in the 1940s.

Over the years there’ve been rumors that a copy might have survived somewhere - especially in Brazil - but nothing credible has ever turned up. At this point, film historians pretty much accept that the original cut is lost forever.

What survives are things like scripts, production stills, editing notes, and studio records, which give people a rough idea of what Welles’s version looked like. And even with all the damage, the released film is still considered a masterpiece. A lot of critics think the original version could’ve stood alongside Citizen Kane as one of the greatest films ever made.
